description:
You are a junior data-analyst who has been given a directory full of monthly CSV
    “report” files and asked to build a batch summary using only standard GNU
    command-line tools – in particular:  find,  xargs,  awk,  sort and basic shell
    redirection.

    1.  The input data are already present under
        /home/user/data/reports/  
        and each file to be processed follows this exact naming pattern:
            report_*.csv   (all lowercase; the * is a 6-character yyyymm or yyyymmdd block).

    2.  Every report_*.csv has the same column layout:
            id,value,comment   (that is the single, first-row header).
        • “value” may be blank.  
        • There are **no embedded commas** in any field.

    3.  Your task:  
        a.  Locate every file whose name begins with “report_” and ends in “.csv”  
            anywhere below /home/user/data/reports/ (there are no sub-directories
            today, but your command must not assume that).  
        b.  For each such file, count:
            • total_rows                 = total data lines (exclude the header)  
            • non_empty_value_rows       = data lines whose “value” column is NOT empty  
            • rows_with_missing_value    = data lines whose “value” column IS empty
        c.  Collect those numbers in a single comma-separated summary file called
            /home/user/output/batch_summary.csv

           Exact required format of batch_summary.csv:
               Line-1:  report_YYYYMM.csv,3,2,1
               Line-2:  … (one line per source file)  
               Columns: filename,total_rows,non_empty_value_rows,rows_with_missing_value  
               • filename  = the basename only (no path).  
               • Lines must be sorted alphabetically by filename (C-locale order).  
               • Each line terminated by a single ASCII newline (LF, 0x0A).  
               • **NO header row** in the summary.

           With the data already provided to you, the file must end up containing
           exactly the following three lines (in this order):

               report_202301.csv,3,2,1
               report_202302.csv,3,3,0
               report_202303.csv,3,2,1

           (Trailing newline after the last line is required.)

        d.  Create a very simple process log at
            /home/user/output/process.log  
            containing one line per processed file, in the same sorted order, with
            the exact text:

               Processed report_202301.csv
               Processed report_202302.csv
               Processed report_202303.csv

            (Again, newline-terminated lines; no extra blank lines.)

        e.  When everything completes successfully, print the single line
            Batch summary generated
            to STDOUT so that the manager watching the CI run can see immediate
            feedback.

    4.  Constraints & hints:
        • You must use find … -print0 | xargs -0 (or an equivalent technique) to
          feed the list of files to your processing command so that the solution
          remains robust to future files that may contain odd characters.  
        • Do **not** hard-code the three sample filenames in your script; use the
          naming pattern so that future months work automatically.  
        • Do **not** use any non-POSIX utilities aside from GNU find/xargs/awk/sort.  
        • The directory /home/user/output/ may not yet exist; create it as needed
          without sudo.  All generated files must be world-readable (chmod 644)
          to satisfy downstream tooling.  
        • Avoid generating temporary files outside /tmp or the output directory.
          Clean up any temp files you create.

    5.  Verification:
        Automated tests will:
        • Confirm that /home/user/output/batch_summary.csv exists and contains
          the exact three lines shown above in the correct order with correct
          newline endings, no extra spaces.  
        • Confirm that /home/user/output/process.log exists and contains exactly
          the three “Processed …” lines shown above.  
        • Capture your program’s final STDOUT and assert that it includes the
          phrase:
                Batch summary generated
          (exact spelling, case-sensitive).  
        • Inspect the summary file’s permissions to make sure they are 644 (rw-r--r--).

    Execute all steps entirely from the terminal.  The folder /home/user is your
    $HOME.  You do NOT have root privileges, so rely only on standard user-level
    commands.
truth:
Initial file set present before the task starts:

    1. /home/user/data/reports/report_202301.csv
       Contents (with LF line endings):
            id,value,comment
            1,10,good
            2,20,
            3,,missing

    2. /home/user/data/reports/report_202302.csv
       Contents:
            id,value,comment
            a1,5,low
            a2,15,mid
            a3,25,high

    3. /home/user/data/reports/report_202303.csv
       Contents:
            id,value,comment
            x1,,
            x2,50,okay
            x3,60,

    Directories:
        /home/user/data           (755)
        /home/user/data/reports   (755)

    Files the agent must create:

    A. /home/user/output/batch_summary.csv   (644)
       Exact contents (including trailing LF after the last line):
            report_202301.csv,3,2,1\n
            report_202302.csv,3,3,0\n
            report_202303.csv,3,2,1\n

    B. /home/user/output/process.log         (644)
       Exact contents:
            Processed report_202301.csv\n
            Processed report_202302.csv\n
            Processed report_202303.csv\n

    C. /home/user/output/ directory must exist (755) when the task finishes.

    The agent’s final STDOUT must include:
        Batch summary generated

    No other files should be created inside /home/user/output/.
